learn.title: Green Hydrogen: PEM vs Alkaline Electrolysis
learn.intro: Green hydrogen is crucial for hard-to-decarbonize sectors like steelmaking and heavy transport. Understanding electrolysis technologies helps investors and policymakers choose the right path for scaling clean hydrogen production efficiently and affordably.
learn.c1_text: Green hydrogen is produced by splitting water using renewable electricity. Unlike grey hydrogen from coal, it releases zero emissions. The process mimics nature's way of creating hydrogen without carbon footprint, making it ideal for sustainable industrial applications.
learn.c2_text: PEM electrolyzers use solid polymer membranes and precious metals like iridium, offering high purity hydrogen and rapid response. Alkaline systems use liquid electrolytes, lower costs, and proven technology but require larger footprints and longer start-up times.
learn.c3_text: India's National Green Hydrogen Mission targets 5 MMT annual production by 2030. The country plans 400 GW renewable capacity to power electrolyzers. Tamil Nadu and Gujarat lead pilot projects, leveraging cheap solar and wind resources for cost-effective green hydrogen production.
learn.stat1: India aims 5 million metric tons of green hydrogen annual production by 2030
learn.stat2: IRENA reports green hydrogen costs could drop to $1.5-2.0/kg by 2030
learn.stat3: Alkaline electrolyzers typically cost $800-1200/kW installed capacity
learn.stat4: PEM systems require 20-30% higher capital investment but offer 2x faster response

learn.title: Wind Resource Assessment Methods
learn.intro: Finding the best wind sites saves millions in project costs and maximizes energy output. Wind resource assessment combines cutting-edge technology with decades of climate data to identify locations where turbines can generate consistent, profitable clean electricity.
learn.c1_text: Wind assessment uses tall met-masts with anemometers to measure speed and direction over 12-24 months. Modern remote sensing LIDAR systems now provide vertical wind profiles from ground-based lasers, reducing installation costs while capturing data up to 200m height.
learn.c2_text: Meteorological databases combine historical weather patterns with real-time measurements. Computational fluid dynamics models predict turbulence and wake effects between turbines. Developers analyze wind shear coefficients typically ranging from 0.1-0.3 for optimal turbine placement.
learn.c3_text: India's wind potential exceeds 1TW, with 70% in offshore areas. Tamil Nadu leads with 35GW installed capacity, followed by Gujarat and Maharashtra. The National Offshore Wind Energy Policy aims 20GW coastal projects by 2030, targeting annual potential of 300BCM wind energy.
learn.stat1: India's current wind capacity stands at approximately 43GW (2024)
learn.stat2: Offshore wind measurement requires specialized floating met-mast platforms
learn.stat3: Typical onshore wind speeds for viable projects exceed 6.5 m/s at hub height
learn.stat4: Wind resource assessment costs range from $50,000-200,000 per site

learn.title: Wave Energy Converter Technologies
learn.intro: Ocean waves contain enormous untapped energy potential. Wave energy converters transform this motion into electricity using innovative mechanical systems. As coastal nations seek diversified renewables, wave power offers predictable generation complementing variable wind and solar sources.
learn.c1_text: Waves carry energy proportional to wave height squared and period. A 2-meter wave with 10-second period contains roughly 50kW per meter of wave crest. converter technologies capture this motion through various mechanisms including point absorbers, oscillating water columns, and overtopping devices.
learn.c2_text: Point absorbers move floating buoys vertically or horizontally. Oscillating water columns use air flow through turbines in enclosed chambers. Attenuators are long floating structures hinged to flex with waves. Overtopping devices store waves in reservoirs for gravity-driven turbines.
learn.c3_text: India explores tidal energy in Gujarat's Gulf of Kutch and Odisha's coast. The 50MW Sardar Sarovar dam generates hydroelectric power with potential for pump storage. India's National Steel Policy emphasizes coastal industries for green hydrogen, supporting future wave energy integration for steelmaking.
learn.stat1: Global wave energy technical potential exceeds 29,500 TWh annually
learn.stat2: US Department of Energy invests $30-50 million annually in marine energy research
learn.stat3: Wave energy converters typically operate at 20-40% efficiency ratios
learn.stat4: India's exclusive economic zone spans 2.3 million square kilometers for ocean energy

learn.title: Electric Vehicle Charging Levels Explained
learn.intro: Understanding EV charging levels prevents range anxiety and optimizes charging schedules. From home outlets to ultra-fast highway stations, each level serves different needs. This knowledge empowers drivers to plan trips effectively and communities to design appropriate infrastructure investments.
learn.c1_text: Level 1 uses standard 120V household outlets providing 2-5 miles of range per hour. Most EVs include Level 1 cables. Overnight charging (8 hours) adds 20-40 miles, sufficient for daily commuting. Ideal for drivers with home parking and predictable schedules.
learn.c2_text: Level 2 operates at 240V like dryers, delivering 10-60 miles per hour. DC fast chargers use 400-800V systems adding 100-300 miles in 30 minutes. Tesla Superchargers achieve 200+ miles in 15 minutes. Higher voltages enable faster electron flow but require specialized equipment and higher utility upgrades.
learn.c3_text: India's AHI targets 25000 public charging stations by 2025. Delhi leads with 4000+ chargers, followed by Karnataka and Maharashtra. The FAME scheme supports 2WIRE charging infrastructure. Mumbai's coastal road project includes 500 fast-charging stations, demonstrating India's rapid EV infrastructure expansion.
learn.stat1: India aims 300,000 electric two-wheelers and 7000 four-wheelers under FAME-II
learn.stat2: Level 1 charging costs approximately $0.03-0.05 per mile equivalent
learn.stat3: DC fast charging can add 100-350 miles of range within 15-30 minutes
learn.stat4: Commercial Level 2 chargers typically cost $3000-7000 installed

learn.title: Solar PV Photoelectric Effect
learn.intro: Solar panels convert sunlight directly into electricity through the photoelectric effect discovered in 1905. This fundamental physics principle enables modern photovoltaics to power homes and businesses. Understanding this process reveals why certain materials and sunlight qualities optimize energy generation.
learn.c1_text: Einstein explained that light arrives as particles called photons carrying energy E=hf. When photons strike semiconductor materials like silicon, they transfer energy to electrons. If photon energy exceeds the material's bandgap (1.1eV for silicon), electrons escape generating electric current.
learn.c2_text: Silicon PV cells contain p-n junctions creating electric fields. Photon absorption generates electron-hole pairs separating at the junction. Metal contacts collect current flow. Modern cells achieve 20-22% efficiency with anti-reflective coatings and passivated emitter designs maximizing photon capture.
learn.c3_text: India's solar capacity reached 72GW by 2024, targeting 500GW renewables by 2030. Gujarat's Charanka solar park spans 5,000 acres with 500MW capacity. The National Solar Mission promotes rooftop installations, aiming 400MW per MW of peak load across states. India now ranks third globally in solar installations.
learn.stat1: India's solar capacity reached 72.1 GW by June 2024
learn.stat2: Silicon solar cells achieve typical efficiencies of 18-22 percent
learn.stat3: One kilowatt-hour of sunlight delivers approximately 4-7 kWh per square meter daily
learn.stat4: India's rooftop solar target equals 400 GW capacity by 2030

learn.title: Renewable Purchase Obligation in India
learn.intro: Renewable Purchase Obligation mandates utilities buy clean energy, driving India's transition. DISCOMs must meet specific renewable energy purchase requirements or face penalties. This policy mechanism ensures grid decarbonization while creating stable markets for renewable developers across the country.
learn.c1_text: RPO targets require utilities purchase minimum renewable energy percentages annually. Central utilities must achieve 40% renewable capacity by 2030. State DISCOMs follow state-specific targets based on installed renewable capacity. Non-compliance attracts penalties up to 1% of turnover monthly.
learn.c2_text: Trading of Renewable Energy Certificates allows compliance flexibility. States conduct quarterly RPO baselining to track progress. Surplus renewable energy can be sold to neighboring states through power exchanges. Violations result in license suspension or cancellation by state electricity regulators.
learn.c3_text: India's Revamped Distribution Sector Scheme addresses DISCOM losses enabling RPO compliance. Tamil Nadu achieved 65% renewable purchase while addressing financial stress. Kerala's 100% RPO compliance demonstrates effective policy implementation. The scheme allocates ₹3.7 lakh crore for infrastructure strengthening and subsidy reforms.
learn.stat1: India's RPO target mandates 40 percent renewable energy by 2030
learn.stat2: Renewable Energy Certificates trade at ₹1,500-2,500 per megawatt-hour in India
learn.stat3: As of 2024, 23 states achieved 100 percent RPO compliance
learn.stat4: India's total electricity consumption reaches 1,700 TWh annually

learn.title: Battery Energy Storage Systems
learn.intro: Battery storage solves renewable energy's intermittency challenge by storing excess power for later use. Grid-scale systems provide essential services like frequency regulation and peak shaving. As costs decline, storage becomes critical for achieving net-zero electricity systems worldwide.
learn.c1_text: Grid-scale batteries provide multiple revenue streams simultaneously. Frequency regulation earns $10-30/kW-monthly. Peak shaving avoids expensive wholesale power purchases. Voltage support services maintain grid stability. These stacked values improve project economics beyond simple energy arbitrage.
learn.c2_text: Lithium-ion dominates with 85% market share offering 4-8 hour duration. Flow batteries like vanadium provide 4-12 hour storage with unlimited cycles. Sodium-ion emerging as lower-cost alternative using abundant materials. Each technology balances capital costs, lifespan, and application suitability differently.
learn.c3_text: India's 2023 PLI scheme supports 50GWh battery manufacturing. Tamil Nadu's 200MWh Horns Rev project demonstrates offshore storage potential. The National Energy Policy emphasizes 100GW storage capacity by 2030. Indian Railways plans 250MWh railway station battery systems reducing diesel generator dependence.
learn.stat1: Global battery storage deployments reached 26 GW by end of 2023
learn.stat2: Lithium-ion battery prices fell 89 percent between 2010 and 2022
learn.stat3: Grid-scale lithium-ion systems typically achieve 85-90 percent round-trip efficiency
learn.stat4: India's production-linked incentive scheme allocates ₹19,500 crore for battery manufacturing
